instruction: Explain perfect forwarding in C++ and show how to implement a factory function that forwards arbitrary arguments to a constructor without unnecessary copies.
output:
<think>
Perfect forwarding preserves the value category (lvalue vs rvalue) of arguments passed through a template function. Without it, forwarding functions always copy or always move, losing the caller's intent. The mechanism relies on: universal references (T&&  where T is a deduced template parameter), reference collapsing rules (T& && -> T&, T&& && -> T&&), and std::forward<T> to cast back to the original category. In a factory function, we want make_unique-like behavior: forward all args to the constructor exactly as the caller provided them. If the caller passes an lvalue, the constructor gets an lvalue reference; if an rvalue, it gets an rvalue reference. Without forward, rvalue arguments would bind to the function parameter (which is itself an lvalue inside the function) and lose their rvalue-ness. I should show the reference collapsing rules explicitly and demonstrate the difference between with and without forwarding.
</think>

Perfect forwarding with universal references:

```cpp
#include <memory>
#include <utility>
#include <iostream>
#include <string>

// Factory that perfectly forwards all arguments to T's constructor
template<typename T, typename... Args>
std::unique_ptr<T> make(Args&&... args) {
    // std::forward<Args>(args)... preserves each arg's value category
    return std::unique_ptr<T>(new T(std::forward<Args>(args)...));
}

// Demonstrating reference collapsing:
// When caller passes lvalue string s:
//   Args deduces to string&
//   Args&& becomes string& && -> collapses to string&
//   forward<string&>(arg) returns string& (lvalue)
//
// When caller passes rvalue string("hello"):
//   Args deduces to string
//   Args&& becomes string&&
//   forward<string>(arg) returns string&& (rvalue)

struct Widget {
    std::string name;
    int value;

    Widget(const std::string& n, int v)
        : name(n), value(v) {
        std::cout << "copy construct: " << name << "\n";
    }

    Widget(std::string&& n, int v)
        : name(std::move(n)), value(v) {
        std::cout << "move construct: " << name << "\n";
    }
};

// Emplace-back pattern: forward into container placement
template<typename Container, typename... Args>
void emplace(Container& c, Args&&... args) {
    c.emplace_back(std::forward<Args>(args)...);
}

// Forwarding through multiple layers
template<typename F, typename... Args>
decltype(auto) invoke_and_log(F&& f, Args&&... args) {
    std::cout << "calling function\n";
    // decltype(auto) preserves the return value category too
    return std::forward<F>(f)(std::forward<Args>(args)...);
}

int main() {
    std::string name = "existing";

    // Lvalue: copies (name is lvalue, forwarded as lvalue)
    auto w1 = make<Widget>(name, 1);

    // Rvalue: moves (temporary, forwarded as rvalue)
    auto w2 = make<Widget>(std::string("temporary"), 2);

    // String literal -> constructs string rvalue -> moves
    auto w3 = make<Widget>("literal", 3);

    // Multi-layer forwarding preserves category
    auto result = invoke_and_log(
        [](std::string&& s) { return s.size(); },
        std::string("test")
    );
}
```

instruction: How do I implement a move-only type in C++ that cannot be copied, and what are the patterns for using it in containers and function returns?
output:
<think>
Move-only types are essential for unique ownership semantics — like unique_ptr, file handles, or socket wrappers. The pattern: delete the copy constructor and copy assignment operator, implement the move constructor and move assignment operator. Key considerations: after move, the source should be in a valid-but-unspecified state (typically nulled out), destructors must handle the moved-from state, and you need to think about self-assignment in move assignment. For containers, std::vector works with move-only types (uses move when resizing), but you can't copy the vector itself. Returning move-only types from functions works via RVO/NRVO (copy elision) or implicit move. std::sort works because it uses std::move internally. I should show a complete RAII wrapper as a move-only type and demonstrate container usage.
</think>

Move-only types with proper RAII semantics:

```cpp
#include <utility>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>
#include <iostream>

// Move-only RAII wrapper for a file descriptor
class UniqueHandle {
public:
    explicit UniqueHandle(int fd = -1) : fd_(fd) {
        if (fd_ >= 0) std::cout << "acquired fd " << fd_ << "\n";
    }

    // Delete copy operations
    UniqueHandle(const UniqueHandle&) = delete;
    UniqueHandle& operator=(const UniqueHandle&) = delete;

    // Move constructor: steal the resource
    UniqueHandle(UniqueHandle&& other) noexcept : fd_(other.fd_) {
        other.fd_ = -1;  // Source is now empty
    }

    // Move assignment: release ours, steal theirs
    UniqueHandle& operator=(UniqueHandle&& other) noexcept {
        if (this != &other) {  // Self-assignment check
            close();
            fd_ = other.fd_;
            other.fd_ = -1;
        }
        return *this;
    }

    ~UniqueHandle() { close(); }

    int get() const { return fd_; }
    bool valid() const { return fd_ >= 0; }

    // Release ownership without closing
    int release() {
        int fd = fd_;
        fd_ = -1;
        return fd;
    }

private:
    void close() {
        if (fd_ >= 0) {
            std::cout << "closing fd " << fd_ << "\n";
            // ::close(fd_); in real code
            fd_ = -1;
        }
    }

    int fd_;
};

// Factory function — RVO elides the move entirely
UniqueHandle open_file(const char* path) {
    int fd = 42; // ::open(path, O_RDONLY);
    return UniqueHandle(fd);  // NRVO or implicit move
}

// Taking ownership via parameter
void consume(UniqueHandle handle) {
    std::cout << "consuming fd " << handle.get() << "\n";
    // handle destroyed at end of scope
}

// Returning optional ownership
UniqueHandle try_open(const char* path) {
    if (path[0] == '/') {
        return UniqueHandle(99);  // RVO
    }
    return UniqueHandle{};  // Empty/invalid
}

int main() {
    // Works in vector — vector uses move internally
    std::vector<UniqueHandle> handles;
    handles.push_back(UniqueHandle(1));       // Move into vector
    handles.push_back(open_file("test.txt")); // RVO + move
    handles.emplace_back(3);                  // Construct in-place

    // Sorting works — std::sort moves elements
    std::sort(handles.begin(), handles.end(),
        [](const UniqueHandle& a, const UniqueHandle& b) {
            return a.get() < b.get();
        });

    // Transfer out of vector
    UniqueHandle taken = std::move(handles.back());
    handles.pop_back();  // Destroys moved-from (no-op, fd=-1)

    // Pass ownership to function
    consume(std::move(taken));
    // taken is now moved-from, valid() returns false

    // Cannot copy:
    // UniqueHandle copy = handles[0];  // ERROR: deleted
    // auto v2 = handles;               // ERROR: can't copy vector of move-only
}
```

instruction: What is RVO/NRVO in C++ and when does it fail? Show examples where copy/move elision applies and where it doesn't, and how to ensure optimal return behavior.
output:
<think>
RVO (Return Value Optimization) and NRVO (Named RVO) are compiler optimizations that eliminate the copy/move when returning objects from functions. Since C++17, RVO for prvalues (temporaries) is guaranteed — it's not an optimization anymore, it's mandatory copy elision. NRVO (named variables) is still optional but almost universally applied. Cases where elision works: returning a temporary directly, returning a named local variable of the same type. Cases where it fails: returning a parameter (not a local), returning one of several possible locals (compiler can't pick which to construct in the return slot), returning a member variable, returning through a conditional where NRVO can't apply, and explicit std::move on a return (actually pessimizes — prevents NRVO). The key rule in C++17: don't std::move the return value when returning a local variable — the compiler applies implicit move if NRVO fails.
</think>

RVO/NRVO: when it works and when it fails:

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
#include <vector>

struct Heavy {
    std::vector<int> data;

    Heavy() : data(1000000) {
        std::cout << "default construct\n";
    }
    Heavy(const Heavy& o) : data(o.data) {
        std::cout << "COPY construct\n";  // We want to never see this
    }
    Heavy(Heavy&& o) noexcept : data(std::move(o.data)) {
        std::cout << "move construct\n";
    }
};

// CASE 1: Guaranteed RVO (C++17 mandatory copy elision)
// Returning a prvalue — no copy, no move, construct directly in caller
Heavy guaranteed_rvo() {
    return Heavy();  // Prvalue: mandatory elision
}

// CASE 2: NRVO — almost always works
// Returning a named local of the return type
Heavy nrvo_works() {
    Heavy h;          // Constructed directly in return slot
    h.data[0] = 42;
    return h;         // NRVO: no copy, no move (usually)
}

// CASE 3: NRVO fails — multiple return paths with different locals
// Compiler can't put both in the return slot
Heavy nrvo_fails_multiple(bool flag) {
    Heavy a, b;
    if (flag)
        return a;  // Can't NRVO both a and b
    else
        return b;  // Implicit move (since C++11)
}

// CASE 4: NRVO fails — returning a parameter
// Parameters have caller-allocated storage, not return slot
Heavy nrvo_fails_param(Heavy h) {
    h.data[0] = 99;
    return h;  // Implicit move, not NRVO
}

// CASE 5: PESSIMIZATION — explicit std::move prevents NRVO!
Heavy pessimized() {
    Heavy h;
    return std::move(h);  // BAD: forces move, prevents NRVO
    // Correct: return h;  // Let compiler apply NRVO or implicit move
}

// CASE 6: std::move IS correct for non-local or different type
std::unique_ptr<Heavy> move_correct() {
    auto ptr = std::make_unique<Heavy>();
    return ptr;  // Implicit move for unique_ptr (NRVO may apply too)
}

// CASE 7: Returning member — no NRVO, needs explicit move
struct Owner {
    Heavy member;

    Heavy take_member() && {  // rvalue-qualified
        return std::move(member);  // Correct: member isn't a local
    }
};

// CASE 8: Guaranteed elision enables move-only prvalue passing
struct NonCopyable {
    NonCopyable() = default;
    NonCopyable(const NonCopyable&) = delete;
    NonCopyable(NonCopyable&&) = delete;  // No move either!
};

NonCopyable magic() {
    return NonCopyable();  // OK in C++17: mandatory elision, no move needed
}

int main() {
    std::cout << "--- guaranteed RVO ---\n";
    auto h1 = guaranteed_rvo();     // 1 construct, 0 copies, 0 moves

    std::cout << "--- NRVO ---\n";
    auto h2 = nrvo_works();         // 1 construct, 0 copies, 0 moves

    std::cout << "--- NRVO fails (multiple) ---\n";
    auto h3 = nrvo_fails_multiple(true);  // 1 construct + 1 move

    std::cout << "--- pessimized ---\n";
    auto h4 = pessimized();         // 1 construct + 1 move (unnecessary!)

    std::cout << "--- non-copyable prvalue ---\n";
    auto h5 = magic();              // Works! No copy or move needed
}
```

instruction: Show me how to implement a type-erased move-only wrapper (like a simplified std::function that only supports move-only callables) using move semantics and small buffer optimization.
output:
<think>
This is an advanced move semantics exercise. std::function requires copyable callables, which is a problem for lambdas capturing unique_ptr. We need a move-only callable wrapper. The design: type erasure via a base class with virtual call/move/destroy, a small buffer optimization (SBO) to avoid heap allocation for small callables, and move-only semantics (deleted copy). SBO stores the callable inline if it fits in a fixed buffer (e.g., 64 bytes), otherwise heap-allocates. The tricky parts: placement new into the buffer, calling the destructor manually, move construction needs to either memcpy the buffer (for trivially movable types) or call the move constructor in the new location. I need a concept/base class that handles the three operations: invoke, move-to-new-location, and destroy. The small buffer needs proper alignment (alignas). This is essentially how std::function implementations work internally.
</think>

Move-only type-erased callable with SBO:

```cpp
#include <cstddef>
#include <memory>
#include <new>
#include <type_traits>
#include <utility>
#include <cassert>

template<typename Signature>
class MoveOnlyFunction;

template<typename R, typename... Args>
class MoveOnlyFunction<R(Args...)> {
    static constexpr size_t BufferSize = 64;
    static constexpr size_t BufferAlign = alignof(std::max_align_t);

    // Type-erased operations vtable
    struct Ops {
        R (*invoke)(void* storage, Args&&... args);
        void (*move)(void* dst, void* src);   // Move-construct at dst from src
        void (*destroy)(void* storage);
        bool is_small;  // In SBO buffer vs heap
    };

    // Generate ops for a specific callable type
    template<typename F, bool Small>
    static const Ops* ops_for() {
        static const Ops ops = {
            // invoke
            [](void* storage, Args&&... args) -> R {
                F& f = Small ? *static_cast<F*>(storage)
                             : **static_cast<F**>(storage);
                return f(std::forward<Args>(args)...);
            },
            // move
            [](void* dst, void* src) {
                if constexpr (Small) {
                    new (dst) F(std::move(*static_cast<F*>(src)));
                    static_cast<F*>(src)->~F();
                } else {
                    // Just move the pointer
                    *static_cast<F**>(dst) = *static_cast<F**>(src);
                    *static_cast<F**>(src) = nullptr;
                }
            },
            // destroy
            [](void* storage) {
                if constexpr (Small) {
                    static_cast<F*>(storage)->~F();
                } else {
                    delete *static_cast<F**>(storage);
                }
            },
            Small
        };
        return &ops;
    }

    alignas(BufferAlign) unsigned char storage_[BufferSize];
    const Ops* ops_ = nullptr;

public:
    MoveOnlyFunction() = default;

    template<typename F,
             typename = std::enable_if_t<!std::is_same_v<std::decay_t<F>, MoveOnlyFunction>>>
    MoveOnlyFunction(F&& f) {
        using Decayed = std::decay_t<F>;
        constexpr bool small =
            sizeof(Decayed) <= BufferSize &&
            alignof(Decayed) <= BufferAlign &&
            std::is_nothrow_move_constructible_v<Decayed>;

        if constexpr (small) {
            new (storage_) Decayed(std::forward<F>(f));
            ops_ = ops_for<Decayed, true>();
        } else {
            auto* ptr = new Decayed(std::forward<F>(f));
            *reinterpret_cast<Decayed**>(storage_) = ptr;
            ops_ = ops_for<Decayed, false>();
        }
    }

    // Move only
    MoveOnlyFunction(const MoveOnlyFunction&) = delete;
    MoveOnlyFunction& operator=(const MoveOnlyFunction&) = delete;

    MoveOnlyFunction(MoveOnlyFunction&& other) noexcept
        : ops_(other.ops_) {
        if (ops_) {
            ops_->move(storage_, other.storage_);
            other.ops_ = nullptr;
        }
    }

    MoveOnlyFunction& operator=(MoveOnlyFunction&& other) noexcept {
        if (this != &other) {
            if (ops_) ops_->destroy(storage_);
            ops_ = other.ops_;
            if (ops_) {
                ops_->move(storage_, other.storage_);
                other.ops_ = nullptr;
            }
        }
        return *this;
    }

    ~MoveOnlyFunction() {
        if (ops_) ops_->destroy(storage_);
    }

    R operator()(Args... args) {
        assert(ops_ && "calling empty MoveOnlyFunction");
        return ops_->invoke(storage_, std::forward<Args>(args)...);
    }

    explicit operator bool() const { return ops_ != nullptr; }
};

// Usage: supports move-only captures!
int main() {
    auto ptr = std::make_unique<int>(42);

    MoveOnlyFunction<int()> fn = [p = std::move(ptr)]() {
        return *p;
    };

    // Can move the function
    auto fn2 = std::move(fn);
    assert(!fn);   // fn is empty after move
    assert(fn2() == 42);

    // Large callable goes to heap
    int big[100]{};
    MoveOnlyFunction<int()> large = [big]() { return big[0]; };
}
```
